Row1.Pathway ID/Name: ROS1 fusion → MAPK/PI3K/STAT signaling (eg, KEGG hsa04010, hsa04151)
Row1.Rationale: ROS1 fusions in NSCLC constitutively activate MAPK, PI3K/AKT and STAT pathways to drive proliferation and survival; repotrectinib potently inhibits diverse ROS1 fusions and resistance mutations (including G2032R, D2033N, L2026M), leading to suppression of these downstream cascades and durable tumor responses in ROS1+ NSCLC, including CNS disease.

Row2.Pathway ID/Name: ROS1 G2032R / D2033N solvent‑front–driven MAPK/PI3K signaling
Row2.Rationale: Solvent‑front mutations such as ROS1 G2032R and D2033N sterically block first‑generation TKIs (crizotinib, entrectinib), maintaining MAPK/PI3K signaling; repotrectinib’s compact macrocycle retains high potency against these mutants in preclinical models and clinical TRIDENT‑1 cohorts, restoring pathway inhibition and clinical benefit after prior ROS1‑TKIs.

Row3.Pathway ID/Name: NTRK fusion–TRK → RAF–MEK–ERK / PI3K–AKT (KEGG hsa04722, hsa04010, hsa04151)
Row3.Rationale: NTRK1/2/3 fusions constitutively activate TRK receptors and RAF–MEK–ERK, PI3K–AKT, and PLCγ signaling; repotrectinib is the most potent inhibitor of wild‑type TRKA/B/C fusions and broadly active against solvent‑front, gatekeeper and xDFG mutants, producing high response rates and prolonged PFS in NTRK+ solid tumors, including NSCLC, in TRIDENT‑1.

Row4.Rationale: On‑target resistance to larotrectinib/entrectinib arises via TRK solvent‑front (e.g., TRKA G595R, TRKC G623R), gatekeeper and xDFG mutations that preserve MAPK/PI3K signaling; repotrectinib’s macrocyclic binding avoids steric clash and maintains low‑nanomolar IC50s against these variants, inducing tumor regressions in TKI‑pretreated NTRK+ patients, including lung cancer.

Row5.Pathway ID/Name: MAPK / PI3K pathway activation in NTRK+ tumors (baseline or acquired)
Row5.Rationale: Exploratory TRIDENT‑1 analyses and other TRK‑TKI series show that some NTRK+ patients progressing on repotrectinib or earlier TKIs harbor baseline or emergent MAPK/PI3K activation and TP53 alterations, supporting TRK‑independent signaling that sustains proliferation despite TRK blockade and shortens response durability.

Row6.Pathway ID/Name: ROS1‑driven MAPK/PI3K/STAT as EGFR‑TKI bypass (EGFR‑mutant → acquired ROS1 fusion)
Row6.Rationale: In EGFR‑mutant NSCLC, acquired ROS1 rearrangements (e.g., EZR–ROS1) restore MAPK/PI3K/JAK‑STAT outputs under EGFR‑TKI pressure, mediating resistance; ROS1‑targeted TKIs (including repotrectinib per NCCN options) can suppress this bypass and achieve meaningful responses, highlighting ROS1 pathway upregulation as a resistance driver and repotrectinib as a rational switch.

Row7.Pathway ID/Name: MET amplification → PI3K/AKT & RAS/MAPK (KEGG hsa04010, hsa04151)
Row7.Rationale: In ROS1+ NSCLC, extrachromosomal MET amplification emerges under entrectinib, driving PI3K/AKT and RAS/MAPK signaling and TKI resistance; similar MET‑mediated bypass has been described after TRK‑TKIs, implying that persistent MAPK/PI3K signaling via MET can limit repotrectinib efficacy and may require combined MET plus ROS1/TRK blockade.

Row8.Pathway ID/Name: EGFR → RAS/RAF/MEK/ERK (KEGG hsa04012) in NTRK1‑rearranged models
Row8.Rationale: In an entrectinib‑resistant NTRK1‑rearranged brain metastasis model, cells with NTRK1‑G595R remained resistant to repotrectinib due to ERK reactivation partly mediated by EGFR; triple inhibition of TRK (repotrectinib), EGFR and MEK resensitized tumors, indicating EGFR‑driven MAPK reactivation as a key bypass limiting repotrectinib monotherapy.

Row9.Rationale: Across TRK‑ and ROS1‑driven models, ERK reactivation via upstream RTKs (EGFR, MET) or other mechanisms can occur despite continued target inhibition, maintaining proliferation; in NTRK1‑G595R M3B cells, ERK reactivation under repotrectinib was necessary for resistance and was overcome only when MEK/ERK were co‑inhibited.

Row10.Rationale: ROS1 and TRK fusions converge on Src–FAK–Rho GTPase and focal‑adhesion pathways that promote survival, migration and brain tropism; ROS1‑TKIs including repotrectinib disrupt these adhesion/motility circuits as part of their antitumor effect, contributing to systemic and intracranial control in ROS1+ and NTRK+ lung cancers.

Row11.Rationale: EMT and developmental pathway activation (often via TGF‑β‑driven PI3K/AKT/mTOR and MEK/ERK) are recognized resistance mechanisms to multiple TKIs in NSCLC; ROS1+ and NTRK+ tumors that undergo EMT can maintain downstream signaling despite kinase inhibition, potentially reducing repotrectinib sensitivity and favoring combination strategies that reverse EMT.

Row12.Rationale: Oncogene‑addicted NSCLCs, including ROS1+ and NTRK+ subsets, typically have low tumor mutational burden and modest PD‑L1 expression, showing limited benefit from immune checkpoint blockade; disease control in these patients relies primarily on sustained inhibition of ROS1/TRK pathways by TKIs such as repotrectinib, with immune pathways becoming targets mainly in post‑TKI combination approaches.
